section.title: Samuel's Farewell
section.sectionRationale: With the king installed, Samuel steps back and gives his farewell as judge. He clears his own name, rehearses the LORD's saving history, and names again the sin of demanding a king. A thunderstorm in the dry season confirms his word, and the terrified people beg for prayer. Samuel answers with grace: though you have sinned, do not turn away; serve the LORD wholeheartedly, for He will not forsake His people for His name's sake, and I will not cease to pray for you. It is one movement: the last judge hands over to the monarchy with a call to fear the LORD.
section.thesis: Even as Israel's sin in demanding a king is exposed and confirmed by a sign from heaven, Samuel proclaims that the LORD, for the sake of His great name, will not abandon the people He was pleased to make His own, so that the way forward under a king is not despair but wholehearted fear of the LORD, sustained by a faithful intercessor.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Samuel clears his name before the LORD and His anointed, verses 1 to 5; the LORD's saving history and Israel's sin recounted, verses 6 to 15; the sign of thunder and the people's fear, verses 16 to 19; and the call not to turn away, grounded in God's faithfulness, verses 20 to 25.

units.label: Samuel clears his name
units.note: bear witness against me before the LORD and His anointed

verses.text: Then Samuel said to all Israel, “I have listened to your voice in all that you have said to me, and I have set over you a king.
Now here is the king walking before you, and I am old and gray, and my sons are here with you. I have walked before you from my youth until this day.

Here I am. Bear witness against me before the LORD and before His {{mashiach|anointed}}: Whose ox or donkey have I taken? Whom have I cheated or oppressed? From whose hand have I accepted a bribe and closed my eyes? Tell me, and I will restore it to you.”
“You have not cheated us or oppressed us,” they replied, “nor have you taken anything from the hand of man.”
Samuel said to them, “The LORD is a witness against you, and His anointed is a witness today, that you have not found anything in my hand.” “He is a witness,” they replied.

units.label: The LORD's saving history and Israel's sin
units.note: the LORD your God was your king

verses.text: Then Samuel said to the people, “The LORD is the One who appointed Moses and Aaron, and who brought your fathers up out of the land of Egypt.
Now present yourselves, so that I may confront you before the LORD with all the righteous acts He has done for you and your fathers.
When Jacob went to Egypt, your fathers cried out to the LORD, and He sent them Moses and Aaron, who brought your fathers out of Egypt and settled them in this place.
But they forgot the LORD their God, and He sold them into the hand of Sisera the commander of the army of Hazor, and into the hands of the Philistines and the king of Moab, who fought against them.
Then they cried out to the LORD and said, ‘We have sinned, for we have forsaken the LORD and served the Baals and Ashtoreths. Now deliver us from the hands of our enemies, that we may serve You.’
So the LORD sent Jerubbaal, Barak, Jephthah, and Samuel, and He delivered you from the hands of your enemies on every side, and you dwelt securely.
But when you saw that Nahash king of the Ammonites was moving against you, you said to me, ‘No, we must have a king to rule over us,’ even though the LORD your God was your king.
Now here is the king you have chosen, the one you requested. Behold, the LORD has placed a king over you.

If you {{yare|fear}} the LORD and serve Him and obey His voice, and if you do not rebel against the command of the LORD, and if both you and the king who rules over you follow the LORD your God, then all will be well.
But if you disobey the LORD and rebel against His command, then the hand of the LORD will be against you as it was against your fathers.

units.label: The sign of thunder and the people's fear
units.note: the LORD sent thunder and rain

verses.text: Now, therefore, present yourselves and see this great thing that the LORD will do before your eyes.
Is it not the wheat harvest today? I will call on the LORD to send thunder and rain, so that you will know and see what a great evil you have committed in the sight of the LORD by asking for a king.”
So Samuel called to the LORD, and on that day the LORD sent thunder and rain. As a result, all the people greatly feared the LORD and Samuel.
They pleaded with Samuel, “Pray to the LORD your God for your servants so that we will not die! For we have added to all our sins the evil of asking for a king.”

units.label: The call not to turn away, grounded in God's faithfulness
units.note: the LORD will not abandon His people, for His great name's sake

verses.text: “Do not be afraid,” Samuel replied. “Even though you have committed all this evil, do not turn aside from following the LORD, but serve the LORD with all your heart.
Do not turn aside after worthless things that cannot profit you or deliver you, for they are empty.
Indeed, for the sake of His great {{shem|name}}, the LORD will not abandon His people, because He was pleased to make you His own.
As for me, far be it from me that I should sin against the LORD by ceasing to pray for you. And I will continue to teach you the good and right way.
Above all, fear the LORD and serve Him faithfully with all your heart; consider what great things He has done for you.
But if you persist in doing evil, both you and your king will be swept away.”

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, messiah. Samuel calls the newly anointed Saul the LORD's anointed and summons him as a witness. Even a flawed king bears this sacred title as God's designated ruler; the term keeps sounding through the book, building toward the true Anointed King, Christ, of whom every anointed one is a shadow.

yare.gloss: To fear, revere, stand in awe. The fear of the LORD is the heart of covenant loyalty, a reverent awe that leads to obedience and trust. Samuel makes it the one thing needful for king and people alike: fear the LORD, serve Him, and all will be well. It is not cringing dread but worshipful reverence that shapes a whole life.

shem.gloss: Name, reputation, revealed character. The LORD will not abandon His people for the sake of His great name; Israel's security rests not on their goodness but on God's commitment to His own honor and covenant. God saves and keeps His people so that His name, His known character as a faithful, saving God, is upheld before the world.

basic.context: Now that Saul is king, Samuel gives his farewell speech as the last judge. First he clears his own record: have I ever cheated or robbed any of you? No, they say. Then he retells Israel's history, how God saved them again and again, and how they still demanded a king even though the LORD was already their King. To drive the point home, Samuel calls on God to send thunder and rain in the dry wheat harvest, and God does, terrifying the people so that they beg for prayer. Samuel answers with astonishing grace: yes, you have sinned, but do not turn away from the LORD. Serve Him with all your heart, for He will not abandon His people for the sake of His great name, and I will keep praying for you. The question is: after such failure, is there hope? Yes, because God is faithful to His own name.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a covenant-renewal speech in the pattern of Moses and Joshua, holding together honest rebuke and real hope. It is not a promise that faithfulness always brings material blessing in a simple way; it lays out the covenant path of blessing and warning. The deepest note is grace: the ground of hope is not Israel's performance but God's commitment to His name. We should read it as a call to wholehearted fear of the LORD and as a revelation that God keeps His people for His own sake.
basic.originalLanguages: Samuel repeatedly calls Saul the LORD's mashiach, His anointed, the sacred title even a flawed king bears. He presses on king and people alike the one essential, to yare, to fear the LORD, a reverent awe that yields obedience and trust. And he anchors all hope not in their goodness but in God's shem, His great name: the LORD will not abandon His people for His name's sake, because He was pleased to make them His own.
basic.typology: Samuel, the blameless leader who intercedes for a sinful people and refuses to stop praying, foreshadows Christ, the sinless Mediator who ever lives to intercede. The covenant hope grounded in God's name, not human merit, points to the gospel, where God saves us for His own glory. And the anointed king who stands as witness anticipates the true Anointed King under whom all is finally set right.
basic.christ: Samuel can find no fault in his own conduct, and he stands and prays for a guilty people, refusing to cease interceding. He is a signpost to Jesus, the truly blameless One, who not only intercedes but bears the guilt Himself and ever lives to pray for us. The hope Samuel offers, that God will not forsake His people for His great name's sake, is secured in Christ, in whom God keeps and saves sinners to the praise of His glory. And the mashiach who stands as witness here points to the Anointed King who will never be swept away.
basic.god: God is the faithful covenant Lord who has saved His people again and again throughout their history. He is holy and just, sending the sign that exposes their sin. Yet He is astonishingly gracious: He will not abandon the people He was pleased to make His own, and He grounds their security in His own name rather than their record. He is the God who keeps His people for His own glory.
basic.crossReferences: Joshua 24 gives a parallel covenant-renewal farewell. Ezekiel 36 says God acts for the sake of His holy name, not Israel's merit. Romans 8 shows Christ interceding for His people. And Philippians 1 assures that God completes the good work He begins.
basic.oneStory: The last judge hands Israel over to the monarchy with honest rebuke and greater hope: God will not abandon His people for His name's sake. That promise, grounded in God's own glory rather than human faithfulness, runs through the whole story to Christ, the blameless Intercessor and true Anointed King, in whom God keeps and saves His people forever.

context.literary: Chapter 12 is Samuel's formal farewell as judge, closing the transition to monarchy that began in chapter 8. It follows the pattern of covenant-renewal addresses like Joshua 24: a review of God's saving acts, an indictment, a call to obedience, and blessings and warnings. It marks the end of the era of the judges and sets the terms under which the monarchy must live.
context.historical: In Israel the wheat harvest fell in the dry season, when rain and thunderstorms were highly unusual, so the sign was unmistakably from God. A leader clearing his name before witnesses was a recognized way to hand over office with integrity. Samuel names past deliverers, Gideon, Barak, Jephthah, and himself, from the era of the judges.
context.canonical: The chapter's teaching that God keeps His people for the sake of His name anticipates Ezekiel 36 and the New Testament gospel of grace. Samuel's ongoing intercession joins him to Moses as a great mediator, Jeremiah 15:1, and points forward to Christ's intercession. The conditional blessings and warnings frame the whole history of the monarchy in Samuel and Kings.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To close the age of the judges with an honest reckoning of Israel's sin in demanding a king, to set the covenant terms for life under the monarchy, and to ground the people's hope not in their own faithfulness but in God's commitment to His great name.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Samuel's covenant address and a specific sign; it does not promise thunderstorms on demand or guarantee simple material reward for obedience. What endures is the call to wholehearted fear of the LORD, the warning against turning to worthless idols, and the assurance that God keeps His people for His name's sake.

debates.question: If asking for a king was such a great evil, why does Samuel still offer hope?

views.label: Grace grounded in God's name, not their merit
views.case: Samuel names the sin plainly, yet the ground of hope is God's own commitment: for the sake of His great name the LORD will not abandon the people He was pleased to make His own; hope rests on God's character, not on undoing the sin.

views.label: The path forward is repentance and wholehearted service
views.case: Though the request was evil, the people can still fear and serve the LORD from the heart; the covenant is not annulled, and genuine, ongoing faithfulness under the king remains open to them by God's mercy.

debates.bearing: Both hold together: sin is real and named, yet God's faithfulness to His name secures a future, calling His people to repent and serve rather than despair, a hope fully secured in Christ.

debates.question: What is the significance of Samuel's refusal to stop praying, verse 23?

views.label: Intercession as a sacred duty
views.case: Samuel regards ceasing to pray for the people as itself a sin against the LORD; the leader's task is to keep interceding and teaching the good way, so that pastoral care continues even after office is handed over.

views.label: A picture of the mediator God provides
views.case: Samuel's steadfast intercession models the mediating role that God graciously supplies for His people, pointing beyond himself to the great and permanent Intercessor, Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way the verse shows that God does not leave His people without a mediator to pray and teach, a mercy that finds its fullness in Jesus, who always lives to intercede.

study.originalLanguages: Three words anchor Samuel's farewell. He twice calls Saul the LORD's mashiach, His anointed, summoning the newly crowned king as a witness; even a king who will fail bears this sacred title as God's designated ruler, and the word keeps sounding through Samuel toward the true Anointed King. Samuel then presses on both king and people the one thing needful, to yare, to fear the LORD: not a cringing dread but a reverent awe that yields obedience and trust, so that if they fear the LORD and serve Him, all will be well. And when he turns from warning to hope, he grounds it not in their goodness but in God's shem, His great name: for the sake of His great name the LORD will not abandon His people, because He was pleased to make you His own. Israel's security rests on God's commitment to His own honor and character as a faithful, saving God, the very ground on which the gospel will one day rest.

typology.type: The blameless leader who intercedes for a sinful people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the sinless Mediator who ever lives to intercede for His people, Hebrews 7:25-26.

typology.type: Covenant hope grounded in God's name, not human merit
typology.fulfillment: The gospel, in which God saves and keeps sinners for the praise of His own glory, Ephesians 1:6 and Ezekiel 36:22-23.

typology.type: The anointed king summoned as witness
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Anointed King, faithful and true, who will never be swept away, Revelation 19:11-16.

study.christ: Samuel stands before Israel and can find no charge against his own conduct, no ox taken, no bribe accepted, and from that place of integrity he pleads for a guilty people and vows never to cease praying for them. He is a bright signpost to Jesus, the truly blameless One, who does more than intercede: He bears the guilt of His people Himself and ever lives to pray for them, Hebrews 7:25. The hope Samuel dares to offer, that the LORD will not forsake His people for the sake of His great name, is made unshakably sure in Christ, in whom God saves and keeps sinners to the praise of His glory, so that our security rests not on our faithfulness but on His. And the mashiach who stands as witness in this chapter, a king who will soon fail, points beyond himself to the Anointed King who is faithful and true and who will never be swept away.
study.god: This chapter reveals the LORD as the faithful covenant God who has saved His people again and again across their history, from Egypt through the judges. He is holy and just, sending thunder and rain out of season to expose the seriousness of Israel's sin and to teach a right fear of Himself. Yet He is astonishingly gracious: even after naming their evil, He will not abandon the people He was pleased to make His own. And, most striking of all, He grounds that faithfulness not in their record but in His own great name, His revealed character as a saving, covenant-keeping God. Israel's hope, and ours, rests on God's commitment to His own glory. Such a God calls His people not to despair over their failures but to fear Him, serve Him wholeheartedly, and rest in His unbreakable faithfulness.

crossReferences.note: Joshua's parallel covenant-renewal farewell, reviewing God's acts and calling Israel to serve the LORD.
God acts for the sake of His holy name, not for Israel's merit, the ground of hope Samuel names.
Christ always lives to make intercession for His people, the fullness of Samuel's ceaseless prayer.
God will complete the good work He began, echoing the promise that He will not abandon His own.

study.oneStory: The last judge hands Israel over to the monarchy with honest rebuke and a greater hope: the LORD will not abandon His people for the sake of His great name. That promise, resting on God's own glory rather than human faithfulness, runs through the whole story to Christ, the blameless Intercessor and true Anointed King, in whom God keeps and saves His people forever. Samuel's ceaseless prayer and his call to fear the LORD point beyond the flawed king Saul to the King who will never fail.

quiz.q: What did Samuel establish at the start of his farewell?

options.text: His own integrity, that he had never cheated, oppressed, or taken a bribe from anyone.
options.note: Verses 3 to 5; Samuel clears his record before the LORD and His anointed.

options.text: That he had installed a king over Israel as they asked.
options.note: He did say this, but the pointed opening was clearing his own name of any wrongdoing.

options.text: That his sons would rule after him.
options.note: The opposite; he had stepped aside and a king now ruled, not his sons.

options.text: That he had grown rich from his years of leadership.
options.note: He proved he had taken nothing; he had not enriched himself.

quiz.q: What sign confirmed Samuel's word about the evil of asking for a king?

options.text: God sent thunder and rain during the dry wheat harvest, when such storms never came.
options.note: Verses 16 to 18; the out-of-season storm was unmistakably from God.

options.text: A sudden storm frightened the people.
options.note: True, but the key was that it came in the dry harvest season, proving it was from God.

options.text: An earthquake shook the assembly.
options.note: The sign was thunder and rain, not an earthquake.

options.text: Fire fell from heaven on an altar.
options.note: The sign here was thunder and rain in the dry season, not fire.

quiz.q: On what did Samuel ground Israel's hope after their sin?

options.text: On God's commitment to His own great name, since He was pleased to make them His own.
options.note: Verse 22; hope rests on God's faithfulness to His name, not Israel's record.

options.text: On the people serving the LORD with all their heart.
options.note: Samuel calls for this, but the ground of hope he names is God's own name and pleasure.

options.text: On Saul proving to be a faithful king.
options.note: Hope rested on God's faithfulness, not on the king, who is also warned.

options.text: On Israel earning back God's favor by good works.
options.note: The hope is grounded in God's grace and name, not in earning favor.

quiz.q: How did Samuel respond to the frightened, repentant people?

options.text: He told them not to turn away but to serve the LORD wholeheartedly, and vowed never to stop praying for them.
options.note: Verses 20 to 23; grace, a call to fear the LORD, and steadfast intercession.

options.text: He urged them to serve the LORD with all their heart.
options.note: True, but omits his gracious reassurance and his vow to keep praying for them.

options.text: He told them their sin was beyond forgiveness.
options.note: The opposite; he offered real hope grounded in God's faithfulness.

options.text: He abandoned them and refused to pray.
options.note: He said it would be sin to cease praying for them.

quiz.q: How does Samuel's farewell point forward to Christ?

options.text: The blameless leader who intercedes and never ceases to pray foreshadows Christ, the sinless Mediator who ever lives to intercede.
options.note: Hebrews 7:25; Samuel's integrity and prayer point to Jesus, who also bears our guilt.

options.text: He shows that leaders should pray for the people they serve.
options.note: True, but it stops short of Christ, the great Intercessor Samuel foreshadows.

options.text: He teaches that God's people can never fall away.
options.note: Samuel warns they can be swept away if they persist in evil; hope rests on God's grace.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The blameless intercessor and the hope grounded in God's name clearly point to Jesus.

questions: Samuel could invite anyone to accuse him and stand clean. Could those who know you best testify to your integrity in money, power, and honesty?
Israel kept forgetting the LORD's past deliverances and reaching for their own solutions. What great things has God done for you that you are tempted to forget?
Samuel grounded hope not in Israel's goodness but in God's great name. How does resting your security on God's faithfulness rather than your performance change the way you face your failures?
Samuel said it would be sin to stop praying for the people. Who has God given you to intercede for, and are you faithful to keep praying for them?
Above all, Samuel said, fear the LORD and serve Him with all your heart. What would it look like this week to let a reverent awe of God shape your whole life?

translationNotes.note: Jerubbaal is Gideon, Judges 6:32; the BSB follows the Septuagint and Syriac in reading Barak and Samuel, where the Hebrew has Bedan and some texts read Samson, all judges God raised up to deliver Israel.
The source has an em dash before even though the LORD your God was your king; rendered here as a comma. This clause names the heart of the sin: they had a King already.
For the sake of His great name, shem, the LORD will not abandon His people; Israel's security rests on God's commitment to His own honor, echoed in Ezekiel 36:22-23.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 12:1-25. One em dash in verse 12 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
